headline: CEX with no public source repo, no recorded audits, and core exchange logic entirely off-chain and unverifiable
short_headline: No repo, no audits, off-chain core

findings.text: Bybit is a centralized exchange whose primary protocol logic runs on proprietary off-chain infrastructure. Its on-chain footprint consists of EOA hot/cold wallets and a small number of multisig addresses — none of which represent deployable smart-contract logic subject to source-code verification in the DeFi sense. A scan of Etherscan for the most-cited Bybit hot-wallet address (0xF977814e90dA44bFA03b6295A0616a897441aceA, a known Binance address often confused; Bybit's known ETH hot wallets such as 0x2B5634C42055806a59e9107ED44D43c426E58258) confirms these are plain EOAs with no associated verified contract source. The February 2025 Bybit hack confirmed that the Safe multisig used for the ETH cold wallet (0x1Db92e2EeBC8E0c075a02BeA49a2935BcD2dFCF4) had its UI compromised, but the Safe contract itself is a standard Gnosis Safe — a third-party open-source contract, not Bybit-authored code. No Bybit-authored deployed contract was found with verified source code attributable to the exchange's core exchange, custody, or settlement logic.

No GitHub repository is recorded for Bybit (protocol.github is blank). No public source-code repository for Bybit's exchange infrastructure, smart contracts, or on-chain components was located during this run. Without a source repo, source-to-repo correspondence cannot be assessed.

No audit links are recorded in protocol.audit_links. Bybit publishes periodic Proof-of-Reserves attestations (most recently via Hacken and Mazars for PoR exercises), but these are asset-balance attestations, not security audits of smart-contract or protocol logic. No smart-contract audit by a recognized firm covering Bybit's own deployed code was located.

Because no audit links are present and no Bybit-authored smart contracts were found, auditor recognition cannot be assessed. The PoR attestations were performed by Hacken (PoR) and Mazars (PoR), neither of which constitutes a Solidity security audit. Hacken is a recognized security firm but the engagements found are PoR attestations only, not code audits.
Post-audit drift cannot be assessed because no baseline audit of Bybit-authored on-chain code exists to compare against.

No Bybit-authored proxy/implementation contract pair was identified. The Safe multisig used as the ETH cold wallet is a standard Gnosis Safe (verified as a third-party contract), not a Bybit-authored proxy. The implementation/proxy question is therefore not applicable to Bybit's own code.

steelman.red: Bybit has no public source code repository, no recorded smart-contract audits, and its entire core exchange and custody logic is off-chain and proprietary — making independent verification of what the 'protocol' actually does impossible by definition.
steelman.orange: Bybit's on-chain footprint is limited to standard third-party contracts (Gnosis Safe multisigs) whose source code is publicly verified, and it publishes periodic Proof-of-Reserves attestations, providing a partial transparency signal even without a full code audit.
steelman.green: All on-chain addresses attributable to Bybit use standard, fully verified third-party contracts (Gnosis Safe), so every deployed bytecode an outsider can interact with is in fact verifiable on the explorer.

rationale.verdict: Choosing red because Bybit's exchange, settlement, and custody logic is entirely off-chain and proprietary with no public source repository and no smart-contract audits recorded. The only on-chain components are EOA wallets and third-party Gnosis Safe contracts not authored by Bybit. The green steel-man is technically true for those third-party contracts but is irrelevant to verifying what Bybit itself does — the core protocol logic cannot be inspected, compiled, or audited by an outsider. The orange steel-man's PoR attestations cover asset balances only, not security or code correctness. Red is the only appropriate grade when there is no public repo and no audit of the entity's own code.

evidence.shows: One of Bybit's publicly cited Ethereum hot wallet addresses — confirmed as a plain EOA with no associated smart-contract source code
Bybit's ETH cold wallet multisig — a standard Gnosis Safe contract (third-party verified code, not Bybit-authored); this was the wallet compromised in the February 2025 $1.5B hack via UI manipulation
Bybit's Proof-of-Reserves page — confirms PoR attestations exist but these are asset-balance exercises, not smart-contract security audits
Hacken's Bybit engagement page — confirms Hacken performed Proof-of-Reserves attestations for Bybit, not a Solidity smart-contract security audit
Bybit's security page — lists security practices and bug bounty reference but no link to a public smart-contract audit report or source repository

unknowns: V2: No GitHub or public source repository for Bybit's exchange or on-chain code was found; source-to-repo correspondence cannot be established for any Bybit-authored contract
V3: No smart-contract audit links are recorded in protocol.audit_links and none were located during this run; only PoR attestations were found
V4: Auditor recognition cannot be assessed due to absence of any smart-contract audit for Bybit-authored code
V5: Post-audit drift cannot be assessed because no audit baseline exists for Bybit-authored on-chain code
V6: No Bybit-authored proxy/implementation pair identified; not applicable to this CEX's on-chain footprint

protocol_metadata.about: Bybit is a centralized cryptocurrency exchange founded in 2018 and headquartered in Dubai, offering spot trading, derivatives (perpetual and futures contracts), options, and earn products across dozens of blockchains. It is one of the largest exchanges globally by derivatives volume and is primarily accessed via its web and mobile trading interface. Unlike DeFi protocols, Bybit's core matching engine, custody, and settlement logic runs on proprietary off-chain infrastructure; its on-chain presence consists of hot/cold wallet addresses used to custody user assets. Bybit suffered a ~$1.5 billion hack in February 2025 when attackers compromised the Safe multisig UI used for its ETH cold wallet.
